nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still surface waters across the lowland tropics of Southeast Asia, including streams, irrigation canals, rice paddies, ponds, and coastal estuaries. Typically found within the uppermost half-metre of the water column, often amid dense marginal or floating vegetation. Some populations inhabit brackish tidal inlets in addition to freshwater.

decorPreferences: floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, salvinia) to provide surface cover and reduce jumping
tight-fitting lid — essential given high escape risk
gentle surface flow or still water
emergent or overhanging vegetation for security

diet.notes: The Halfbeak is an obligate surface feeder in the wild, hunting small invertebrates at or just beneath the water surface. In the aquarium it does best on live and frozen prey such as fruit flies (Drosophila), small crickets, mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Many specimens initially refuse prepared dry foods, though some individuals can be gradually conditioned to accept small floating pellets or micro-sticks. Regardless, a regular offering of live or frozen food should always be maintained. Feed small amounts two to three times daily and remove any uneaten live food promptly. Sinking foods are largely ignored by this species, which rarely descends below the surface film.

goodWith: peaceful mid- and bottom-dwelling fish of similar size (corydoras, small rasboras, ember tetras)
small gouramis that do not compete at the surface
other halfbeaks in a well-sized tank with visual breaks
avoidWith: other male Wrestling Halfbeaks in small tanks (jaw-wrestling and fin damage)
fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — long lower jaw is vulnerable
large or aggressive fish that cause chronic stress
boisterous surface feeders that out-compete for food (hatchetfish in large numbers)
Celebes Halfbeak (Nomorhamphus liemi, fw-fish-294) — different genus and care requirements; mixing invites confusion and potential aggression

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by OpenCage (opencage.info), ~2483px. Sharp full side profile of a single Dermogenys pusilla clearly showing the dramatically elongated lower jaw with yellow/red tip; whole fish in frame, well lit.

Photo by -serwacy01- (iNaturalist), large size. Crisp surface shot with mirror reflection; vivid red-tipped anal and caudal fins and the long lower beak clearly visible on a vibrant, in-colour specimen.

Photo by Ahsan al hidayat, ~2092px. Two individuals at the water surface, both showing the diagnostic red-tipped elongated lower jaw; represents the shoaling surface habit, though slightly soft through the surface film.

summary: The Halfbeak (Dermogenys pusilla) is a slender, surface-dwelling livebearer from Southeast Asia, recognised by its dramatically elongated lower jaw. Males are known to interlock jaws in wrestling contests, a behaviour historically exploited in parts of the region much like fighting fish. The species is a demanding surface feeder with a strong tendency to jump.
careNotes:
The Halfbeak (Dermogenys pusilla) is a slender, viviparous species found across the slow-moving lowland waters of Southeast Asia, from Thailand to the Indonesian archipelago. It occupies the surface film almost exclusively, and the aquarium should reflect this: ample floating plant cover — frogbit, salvinia, and water lettuce all work well — provides security, breaks line-of-sight between rival males, and helps reduce jumping. A tightly-fitting lid is essential; halfbeaks are persistent jumpers and will find any gap. A tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint is the practical minimum, with larger tanks significantly reducing male–male aggression. Water should be warm (24–28°C), neutral to slightly alkaline (pH 7.0–8.0), and moderately hard. Some keepers add a small amount of marine salt (to around SG 1.001–1.003) to recreate the brackish coastal conditions found in some wild populations, which may help reduce disease incidence, but this is not mandatory.

Feeding is the most demanding aspect of care. The Halfbeak is an obligate surface feeder, and many specimens refuse dry foods entirely on arrival. A diet of live and frozen small invertebrates — Drosophila fruit flies, mosquito larvae, daphnia, small brine shrimp, and bloodworms — should be offered from the outset. Some individuals can be gradually conditioned to accept small, high-quality floating pellets, but a live-food supplement should always be part of the routine. Food should be offered at the surface; sinking foods are generally ignored.

Male–male aggression centres on jaw-wrestling: males interlock their elongated lower mandibles and push against each other, a behaviour historically exploited in parts of Southeast Asia much as fighting fish contests are. In a well-decorated, spacious tank, a ratio of one male to two or three females is recommended. Multiple males can coexist if the tank is large enough and sight-lines are broken with floating vegetation. The Halfbeak is a different species from the Celebes Halfbeak (Nomorhamphus liemi, fw-fish-294), which belongs to a distinct viviparous genus endemic to Sulawesi and has somewhat different care requirements; the two are sometimes confused in the trade due to similar names.
breedingNotes: The Halfbeak is viviparous, meaning females give birth to fully formed live young rather than laying eggs. Gestation lasts approximately four to six weeks, with broods typically ranging from five to twenty-five fry. Newborns arrive at around 8 to 10 mm in length and are capable of taking micro-live foods — such as newly hatched brine shrimp, micro-worms, and Drosophila larvae — from birth. Females can produce consecutive broods without additional mating once fertilised. Parental predation of fry is possible, so dense floating plant cover or a separate rearing tank significantly improves survival rates. Sexing adults is straightforward: males are smaller and slimmer, and possess a modified anal fin called the andropodium, which is used for internal fertilisation; females are larger-bodied and develop a visible darkened gravid spot when carrying young. Maintaining a ratio of one male to two or three females helps reduce male harassment of gravid females. Conditioning both sexes on live foods such as mosquito larvae, daphnia, and fruit flies before and during breeding tends to improve fry yield.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dermogenys pusilla
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (SE Asia: Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Myanmar, Vietnam, Philippines), water parameters (temperature 22–28°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 90–268 ppm / ~5–15 dH), adult size (males to 4 cm, females to 7 cm), habitat (surface of slow/still waters, often in brackish conditions), diet (surface insects, small invertebrates), breeding notes (viviparous, 5–25 fry, 4–6 week gestation), and male aggression/jaw-wrestling behaviour. Taxonomy: Zenarchopteridae, Beloniformes confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Dermogenys pusilla
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Beloniformes / Zenarchopteridae), adult size (to 8.5 cm TL recorded but typical aquarium specimens 5–7 cm), native range (peninsular SE Asia, Malay Archipelago), habitat (freshwater and brackish coastal lowland streams), and diet (surface invertebrates). adultSizeCm set to 4–7 cm per SeriouslyFish sex-differentiated max; FishBase TL maximum of 8.5 cm represents large wild females.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Halfbeak Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care summary. Confirmed surface-insectivore feeding behaviour, high escape risk, lid requirement, floating plant recommendations, and brackish salt tolerance. Cited 10-gallon (38 L) minimum for small groups; 60 L used in this file as a more robust minimum per SeriouslyFish guidance and male aggression considerations.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Halfbeak profile
additional.notes: Supplementary data: confirmed jaw-wrestling behaviour and historical use as fighting fish in SE Asia, care level intermediate, live food dependency, viviparous reproduction, and distinction from Nomorhamphus (Celebes Halfbeak). Temperature range 24–28°C and brackish tolerance also noted.

additional.name: FishBase — Nomorhamphus liemi (Celebes Halfbeak, fw-fish-294)
additional.notes: Reference cross-check to distinguish D. pusilla from the Celebes Halfbeak (Nomorhamphus liemi). Nomorhamphus liemi is endemic to Sulawesi, belongs to a separate genus within Zenarchopteridae, and has slightly different care requirements (cooler water, 22–26°C). Cited in avoidWith and careNotes to disambiguate the two species sold under similar names.

fish.breedingNotes: Viviparous livebearer with a gestation period of approximately four to six weeks and broods of five to twenty-five fry. Fry are born at around 8 to 10 mm and will accept newly hatched brine shrimp from birth. Condition breeders on live foods before and during breeding. Dense floating plant cover or a separate rearing tank is recommended to protect fry from parental predation. A ratio of one male to two or three females reduces harassment of gravid females.
